title: Loading a Saved Model
concept: Loading a serialized model means reading the saved file back into a server's memory, instantly recreating the trained model without needing to retrain or access the original data.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why is joblib often chosen over pickle for large tree-based models?

options.text: It's optimized for large numeric arrays, making it faster and more efficient.
It's the only format that works in Python.

exercises.explain: Correct! Joblib is built for efficiently handling large numeric arrays.

cards.title: Thawing the frozen sculpture
cards.body: Remember our frozen ice sculpture from serialization? Loading is the reverse: taking that frozen file and instantly bringing it back to a fully usable, active state — ready to make decisions again, without needing to carve it from scratch.

cards.title: Loading: Reactivating the model
cards.body: Loading a model means reading a serialized file from disk and reconstructing it back into an active object in a program's memory (RAM), ready to accept new inputs and return predictions instantly.

cards.title: Loading a saved video game
cards.body: Loading a model is exactly like loading a saved video game file. You don't have to replay the entire game from the beginning (retrain from scratch) — you instantly jump back to exactly where you left off (the fully trained state), ready to keep playing.

cards.title: The server startup sequence
cards.body: When a web server starts, it runs a short loading script: 1. Find the saved model file on disk, 2. Read the file into memory using the matching library (joblib or pickle), 3. Keep the loaded model ready in memory so it can instantly respond to incoming prediction requests.

cards.title: Loading doesn't require the original training data
cards.body: You might think a server needs access to the millions of original training rows to make predictions. In reality, once a model is loaded from its serialized file, it only needs the new input it's asked to predict on — the original training dataset is completely unnecessary at this stage.

cards.title: Save and Load Cycle
cards.body: The full round trip of a model's lifecycle:

left.text:
Trained model in RAM
-> Written to a file
-> Stored on disk.

right.text:
File read from disk
-> Reconstructed in RAM
-> Ready to predict instantly.

exercises.prompt: What happens when a server 'loads' a serialized model?

options.text: The saved file is read from disk and reconstructed into an active object in memory, ready to predict.
The model retrains itself from scratch using new random data.
The original training dataset is automatically downloaded.
The model file is permanently deleted from disk.

exercises.explain: Correct! Loading reconstructs the saved file into a usable object in active memory.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A server needs access to the original training dataset in order to load a saved model and make new predictions.
exercises.explain: False! Once serialized and loaded, a model only needs new input data — the original training set isn't required.

exercises.sentence: ___ a model means reading its saved file back into active memory, ready to predict.
exercises.explain: Loading reactivates a serialized model, bringing it back into working memory.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario correctly describes a server loading a saved model?

options.text: A web server starts up, reads 'price_model.joblib' from disk into memory, and is instantly ready to answer prediction requests.
A web server retrains a brand-new model from scratch every time a user visits the website.
A web server downloads the entire original 5-million-row training dataset before answering any requests.

exercises.explain: Correct! Reading the file into memory and being instantly ready is exactly what loading a model looks like.

exercises.prompt: A mobile app needs to predict whether a photo contains a specific object, using a pre-trained classical model. Which approach is correct for deploying this on a server?

options.text: Serialize the trained model once, then have the server load that saved file into memory on startup to answer prediction requests instantly.
Retrain the entire model from scratch every time a new photo needs a prediction.
Store the original training images on the server and manually compare each new photo to all of them.
Skip serialization entirely and keep the model only in the developer's personal laptop memory.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Serialize once, then load the saved file on the server — this is the standard, efficient pattern that avoids costly and unnecessary retraining. Next, let's look at a tricky issue that can trip up this process: portability across different computers!
